title: Dictionary Entry vs Dictionary Override
question: What is the difference between a dictionary entry and a dictionary override?
summary: Explains baseline field definition versus table-specific override behavior.
exactAnswer: A dictionary entry defines the base metadata for a field. A dictionary override lets a child table override specific field properties inherited from a parent table without redefining the entire field on the child table.
keyPoints: Use overrides when a child table needs different mandatory, read-only, or choice behavior.
Do not create redundant duplicate fields when inheritance already provides the field.
Dictionary overrides are common on task-derived child tables.
examples: Make a task field mandatory on incident only by using a dictionary override.
Change the label of an inherited field on a child table where appropriate.

title: Import Set vs Transform Map
question: What is the difference between an import set and a transform map?
summary: Explains staging versus mapping during data ingestion.
exactAnswer: An import set is the staging table where inbound data is first loaded. A transform map defines how the data in that staging table is mapped, transformed, coalesced, and written into the target table.
keyPoints: Transform map = mapping and data movement logic.
Use transform scripts when field mapping alone is not enough.
examples: Load CSV rows into an import set table first.
Use a transform map to move user records into sys_user.

title: Coalesce in Transform Maps
question: What does coalesce do in a transform map?
exactAnswer: Coalesce identifies whether an incoming row should update an existing target record or create a new one by matching one or more fields marked for coalescing. Choosing the right coalesce field is critical for preventing duplicates.
keyPoints: Coalesce is the record matching rule for the transform.
Poor coalesce choice leads to duplicates or wrong updates.
Use a unique, stable identifier when possible.
examples: Use user_name as coalesce for sys_user imports if it is unique and stable.
Use a discovery or external ID carefully for CMDB imports.

title: CI Relationship vs Reference Field
question: What is the difference between a CMDB relationship and a simple reference field?
summary: Clarifies modeling dependencies in CMDB design.
exactAnswer: A reference field points from one record to another for a direct field-level association, while a CMDB relationship models a typed dependency between CIs using relationship records. CMDB relationships are used for service impact analysis, topology, and dependency mapping, not just simple lookup convenience.
keyPoints: Reference fields are not a substitute for proper dependency modeling.
CMDB relationships carry relationship type and directional meaning.
Service Mapping and impact analysis rely on relationship data.
examples: A server record may reference a support group via a reference field.
An application service 'depends on' a database server through a CMDB relationship.

title: Purpose of the Identification and Reconciliation Engine
question: What does IRE do in CMDB?
summary: Explains duplicate prevention and source governance in CMDB data ingestion.
exactAnswer: IRE identifies whether an incoming CI payload matches an existing CI and then applies reconciliation rules to determine whether the source is allowed to update specific attributes. It is central to reducing duplicates and preserving source-of-truth governance in the CMDB.
keyPoints: Identification decides which CI the payload belongs to.
Reconciliation decides whether the source can update the attribute values.
Interview trap: duplicate prevention is not only about coalesce; IRE is the CMDB-specific control model.
examples: Discovery may identify a CI and update allowed attributes.
A lower-priority source may match a CI but be blocked from overwriting governed fields.

title: MID Server Basics
question: What is a MID Server and when is it required?
summary: Explains the role of the MID Server in discovery and integrations.
exactAnswer: A MID Server is a Java-based agent installed within the customer network that enables secure communication between the ServiceNow instance and resources that are not directly reachable from the cloud instance. It is commonly required for Discovery, Service Mapping, and some integration patterns involving internal systems.
keyPoints: MID Servers allow outbound communication from the network to the instance model.
Discovery depends heavily on a healthy MID Server setup.
Credential, capability, and ECC Queue troubleshooting are common interview themes.
examples: Discover Windows and Linux servers on-premises.
Call an internal API that is not exposed publicly.
